title: The Key to Happiness: Overcoming Destructive Beliefs
description: This article explains how destructive beliefs shape the way we see ourselves and the world, and how they get in the way of a fulfilling life. Drawing on the principles of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), it explores the difference between temporary relief and real change that happens when we rethink our usual thoughts and core beliefs. It also introduces practical tools, such as the Dysfunctional Attitude Scale, that help identify and adjust these patterns and build lasting emotional well-being.

title: “I Did Great!” Why Am I Always Waiting for Praise?
description: This article explores how dependence on other people’s approval makes us vulnerable to their opinions and gets in the way of stable self-esteem. We look at how underlying beliefs shape emotions and your sense of self-worth, and why self-validation matters more than external praise. At the end, you’ll find practical steps to help you rethink your need for approval and strengthen your inner foundation.

title: The Path to Healthy Self-Esteem
description: This text explores how to free yourself from relying on other people’s approval. The main steps include weighing the pros and cons of your current beliefs, rewriting unhelpful assumptions into more realistic and supportive ones, and building the habit of self-approval. By following these recommendations, you can become more independent and improve your quality of life.

title: Love Addiction
description: Love, celebrated by poets and artists, can lift us up and give life a sense of fullness. But when love turns into a compulsive need, it erodes our sense of self and makes it hard to build healthy relationships, especially for people with low self-esteem. Lasting happiness and a meaningful life become possible when we learn to regulate our mood and enjoy sources of pleasure that do not depend on having a partner.

title: How to Overcome Love Addiction?
description: This article explores happiness and beliefs related to love and relationships. It explains that happiness can be achieved in various ways and offers techniques to change negative beliefs associated with loneliness. The article teaches how to find joy in everyday things and develop independently of romantic relationships.

title: Achievement Addiction
description: Love can inspire, give strength, and fill life with meaning. But when it turns into a need you can’t feel whole without, it starts to erode you and gets in the way of healthy relationships. The same thing happens with achievement addiction. Lasting happiness becomes possible when you learn to lean not only on success, but also on your feelings, values, and your ability to find joy beyond results.

title: How to Stop Living for Achievements?
description: The article discusses the dangers of being addicted to success, pointing out that achievements do not guarantee happiness and well-being. Examples of celebrities like Lady Gaga and Jim Carrey illustrate that even the most successful people can suffer from depression and other issues. The importance of finding a balance between goals and personal life, as well as recognizing the intrinsic value of each person regardless of their achievements, are the key takeaways.

title: Is It Always My Fault? The Illusion of Omnipotence
description: This article explores the psychological phenomenon of personalization—the belief that you are the cause of everything that happens around you. It explains how this thinking style works and how it leads to stress, anxiety, low self-esteem, and difficulties in relationships. The article also looks at where this belief comes from, starting with childhood experiences and cultural influences, and offers recommendations for overcoming personalization and freeing yourself from unnecessary guilt.

title: The Escape from the Trap of “It’s All My Fault”
description: The article examines the phenomenon of personalization—the tendency to perceive events as personally related to oneself. It discusses methods to reduce the negative consequences of such thinking, including self-observation and situational analysis. The author offers practical advice and exercises to improve mental well-being and free oneself from unnecessary guilt.

title: Being Unhappy: A Matter of Choice or Circumstance?
description: This article explores the idea that genuine happiness depends less on external circumstances and more on our inner perspective and personal choices. It emphasizes that clinging to high expectations and external factors makes us vulnerable to dissatisfaction, whereas accepting reality and practicing gratitude support more stable, lasting happiness. The core message is that recognizing your own responsibility for your happiness and emotional state is a key step toward a fuller, more satisfying life.

title: How to Stop Being a Victim and Take Responsibility for Your Happiness?
description: The article encourages abandoning the role of a victim of circumstances and taking responsibility for your life by controlling your thoughts and actions. The author suggests practicing journaling to identify limiting beliefs and develop proactive thinking through the right questions. Transitioning to an active position in life requires patience, mindfulness, and gradual achievement of realistic goals.

title: The World Owes Me: What’s Wrong with This Belief?
description: This article explores the mindset of “the world owes me”, which often develops in people who expect life to revolve around them and meet all their needs. The author explains how this belief starts in childhood, shows how it manifests in adult life, and describes its negative consequences, including disappointment, tension in relationships, and loss of independence. In conclusion, the article highlights the importance of accepting the world as it is and recognizing that there is room in it not only for us, but for other people as well.

title: How to Cope with Obligations?
description: The article discusses the beliefs of obligations as a source of stress and dissatisfaction, emphasizing that these attitudes make us feel like prisoners of circumstances. The author suggests recognizing the unrealistic nature of such beliefs, replacing them with more flexible formulations, and taking responsibility for our desires and actions. The practice of gratitude and compassion, as well as the ability to ask for help and accept imperfections, can help reduce stress and improve the quality of life.

title: Addicted to the Ideal: Perfectionism
description: This article explores perfectionism, emphasizing that striving for high standards can be helpful, but when it turns into rigid demands, it leads to negative consequences such as anxiety and chronic dissatisfaction. Real-life examples show how early experiences and upbringing can contribute to the development of perfectionistic beliefs and behaviors. The article concludes with an invitation to find a balance between aiming for excellence and accepting a "good enough" result in order to avoid burnout and disappointment.

title: What to do if you have perfectionism, no ideal, but want to live normally?
description: The article examines two types of perfectionism - negative and positive, offering effective recommendations for dealing with perfectionist tendencies. The author presents eight key strategies, including motivation for change, cognitive restructuring, process orientation instead of result orientation, and formation of new, healthier beliefs. The main idea is that perfectionism can be overcome by learning to accept imperfection, love oneself unconditionally, and take care of oneself daily.
